comments:
Summary:
This paper studies recovery of binary vertex labels from noisy pairwise (edge) measurements and noisy vertex-side information on sparse graphs. Building on Model 1 (edge flips with probability p, vertex flips with probability q), the authors present: (i) an efficient, statistically optimal message-passing algorithm for trees achieving Õ(p n) Hamming error; (ii) a general TreeDecompositionDecoder that selects a subset of edges to obtain a small-treewidth subgraph, performs local edge-MLE per component (up to sign) and then combines components using vertex side information and ERM-style arguments; and (iii) upper and matching lower bounds for several graph families (thin/constant-height grids, hypergrids, ring lattices, Newman–Watts small-world). The analysis leverages concentration (entropy method), combinatorial cut arguments, and fast-rate excess-risk bounds from statistical learning theory.

Strengths:
- The paper presents a coherent, well-motivated algorithmic framework that cleanly separates edge-based local inference and global sign selection using vertex observations. This combination of tree-decomposition techniques with ERM-style statistical analysis is elegant.
- Theoretical results are fairly comprehensive: finite-sample bounds (with high probability), computational cost analysis (dependent on treewidth / component size), and matching lower bounds for multiple graph families. The work meaningfully extends prior results (e.g., Globerson et al.) to non-planar graphs and constant-height grids.
- Proof techniques are appropriate and nontrivial (use of entropy method for dependent component errors, fast-rate learning bounds to translate sign-class size to Hamming error), and the paper discusses robustness to a semi-random model.

Weaknesses / Limitations:
- The guarantees rely on having an admissible tree decomposition (or being able to delete edges to obtain a small-treewidth subgraph). While the authors construct such decompositions for the graph families of interest, the paper acknowledges (Section 6) that finding a good E' / decomposition automatically is nontrivial; the practical impact of this limitation is not fully explored. More discussion or an algorithmic recipe for obtaining E' given general graphs would strengthen applicability.
- Several important quantities and constants are hidden in Õ notation (log factors and constants depending on degrees/tree parameters). Explicit dependence on q (beyond q bounded away from 1/2) and on constants in the rates would help assess practical regimes.
- The approach requires components of constant (or logarithmic) size to remain computationally feasible; for larger treewidth graphs this may be infeasible. The paper mentions possible substitution of other subroutines (e.g., spectral) but provides no worked examples with such hybrids.
- No experimental/empirical validation is provided. While the paper is largely theoretical, some small simulations (e.g., grids, rings, Newman–Watts) would illustrate constants and practical performance versus theory and previous methods.

Suggestions for improvement:
1. Add a dedicated discussion or a small algorithm for finding (or approximating) a good edge subset E' and tree decomposition in general graphs; quantify how suboptimal edge deletion affects error rates.
2. Provide more explicit constants in main theorems (or a short paragraph explaining the magnitude of the hidden constants) and dependence on q so readers can judge practical parameter ranges.
3. Include small-scale experiments to demonstrate the algorithm’s behavior (computation time, empirical Hamming error) on representative graphs and compare to existing baselines where possible.
4. Clarify a couple of technical places where steps are abbreviated (e.g., in transforming component-wise failures to signer-class size and the subsequent ERM bounds) to aid readers following the proofs.

Overall assessment:
This is a solid theoretical contribution that broadens understanding of partial recovery with side information on sparse/non-expanding graphs. The combination of combinatorial and learning-theoretic analyses is novel and the results are relevant to inference on structured graphs. I recommend acceptance (or, given this is arXiv, strong endorsement for dissemination) after addressing clarity and discussion points above.
